title: Te Kōrero o Te Awa: Voice of the River / Ngā Reo o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore how the stories of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (our local stream) have been told through generations by Ngāti Kahungunu. They will examine traditional oral narratives, contemporary written accounts, and environmental reports to understand how language shapes our relationship with this significant waterway that flows through our college grounds and sustains our community.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare at least three different textual forms (oral tradition, written narrative, scientific report) that document the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Create a multimodal text that combines written, oral, and visual elements to tell a contemporary story about the awa that honors Ngāti Kahungunu perspectives
Apply critical literacy skills to evaluate how different authors and speakers use language to position readers/listeners in relation to the awa
Demonstrate understanding of Māori cultural concepts (particularly kaitiakitanga and whakapapa) through written and oral responses to texts about the awa

activities.title: Whakarongo: Listening to the Voices of the Awa
activities.description: Students will listen to recorded oral histories from Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, followed by a guided walk along the awa where they will record their own observations and reflections. They will analyze the language features used in the oral histories and their own notes.

activities.title: Tuhituhi: Mapping the Narrative Journey
activities.description: Using historical documents from the local marae archives and contemporary environmental reports, students will create comparative text analysis maps showing how language choices reflect different perspectives, purposes, and cultural contexts in writing about the awa.

activities.title: Kōrerorero: Dialogue with Kaitiaki
activities.description: Students will prepare and conduct interviews with local kaitiaki (environmental guardians) from Ngāti Kahungunu, focusing on how they use language to communicate the importance of the awa. They will transcribe and analyze the interview language features.

activities.title: Waihanga: Creating Our River Stories
activities.description: Working in small groups, students will create multimodal narratives about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku that blend written text, recorded oral narration, and visual elements, ensuring their work reflects both personal connection and cultural understanding.

activities.title: Whakaatu: Sharing Our Mahi
activities.description: Students will present their multimodal narratives to an audience that includes kaumātua, family members, and local kaitiaki, explaining their language choices and how their work connects to both the English curriculum and Ngāti Kahungunu values.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and kaitiaki as guest speakers and knowledge sharers
Archival oral history recordings from Heretaunga Ararau (Ngāti Kahungunu cultural archive)
Local environmental monitoring reports from Hawke's Bay Regional Council on Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Digital recording equipment and editing software for creating multimodal texts

tasks: Comparative analysis essay examining how language choices in two different text types (e.g., oral history vs. scientific report) shape understanding of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Multimodal narrative creation with accompanying reflective commentary explaining language choices and cultural considerations
Oral presentation demonstrating understanding of how language reflects and shapes relationships with significant places

nzcAlignment: English: Processes and strategies - Using a range of processing and comprehension strategies with understanding and confidence
English: Ideas - Show developed understanding of ideas within, across, and beyond texts
English: Language features - Show understanding of how language features are used for effect within and across texts
English: Structure - Show understanding of how text conventions work together to create meaning and effect
Social Sciences: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
